coredata.dc:title: Carbon-dot-based colorimetric turn-on probe for selectively detecting thiophenols
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Shengrui
Wang, Qin
Liu, Zhifeng
Liu, Jin
Lu, Jiufu
coredata.dc:description: A colorimetric turn-on probe was developed for the selective naked-eye determination of thiophenols by using yellow-emissive carbon dots (Y-CDs) as the colorimetric reporter and 2, 4-dinitrobenzenesulfonamide (DNBS) group as the recognition unit. The surfaces of Y-CDs were covalently coated with 2, 4-dinitrobenzenesulfonyl chloride to afford the 2, 4-dinitrobenzene- functionalized carbon dots (CD-DNBS) as a novel probe for detecting thiophenols. CD-DNBS was nearly colorless. Upon addition of thiophenol, the DNBS moiety of CD-DNBS could be readily cleaved via nucleophilic substitution reactions, leading to a dramatic increase in absorbance at 400 nm. The established detection probe displays a high selectivity towards thiophenol over biothiols and other biologically related species.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon materials
Sensors
Nanoparticles

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram for the preparation of CD-DNBS and its role in PhSH detection.
XPS survey scan (A), high-resolution XPS survey scans of N 1 s (B) and S 2p (C) and FTIR characterization (D) of CD-DNBS.

(A) Time courses of changes in the absorbance of CD-DNBS (20 μg/mL) incubated with 20 μM PhSH. (B) Responses of CD-DNBS to different concentrations (from bottom to top, 0, 1, 2, 6, 8, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35, 40, 45, 50, 55, 60, 65 and 70 μM) of PhSH. (C) Linear correlation between the absorbance and PhSH concentrations, inset shows the absorbance at 400 nm as a function of PhSH concentration. (D) Absorption spectra of the Y-CDs and final product, inset shows the fluorescence spectra (λex = 435 nm) of CD-DNBS (20 μg/mL) incubated with 50 μM PhSH.

(A) Changes in the absorbance of CD-DNBS (20 μg/mL) after incubated with PhSH and other biological species for 10 min. (B) Photographs of CD-DNBS solutions and color alteration of CD-DNBS-coated filter paper after immersing into different concentrations (0, 0.5, 1 and 2 μM) of PhSH.

head.ce:title: Carbon-dot-based colorimetric turn-on probe for selectively detecting thiophenols

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shaanxi Key Laboratory of Catalysis, School of Chemistry and Environment Science, Shaanxi University of Technology, Hanzhong, Shaanxi 723000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Environment Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shaanxi Key Laboratory of Catalysis, School of Chemistry and Environment Science, Shaanxi University of Technology, Hanzhong, Shaanxi 723000, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A colorimetric turn-on probe was developed for the selective naked-eye determination of thiophenols by using yellow-emissive carbon dots (Y-CDs) as the colorimetric reporter and 2, 4-dinitrobenzenesulfonamide (DNBS) group as the recognition unit. The surfaces of Y-CDs were covalently coated with 2, 4-dinitrobenzenesulfonyl chloride to afford the 2, 4-dinitrobenzene- functionalized carbon dots (CD-DNBS) as a novel probe for detecting thiophenols. CD-DNBS was nearly colorless. Upon addition of thiophenol, the DNBS moiety of CD-DNBS could be readily cleaved via nucleophilic substitution reactions, leading to a dramatic increase in absorbance at 400 nm. The established detection probe displays a high selectivity towards thiophenol over biothiols and other biologically related species.

ce:section.ce:para: Thiophenols can exhibit higher toxicity than aliphatic thiols and cause numerous health problems, including nervous system damage, coma, and even death [1,2]. Considering their highly toxic properties, a number of methods, such as chromatography, fluorometry, have been proposed for selective detection of thiophenols [3,4]. However, these strategies rely on cost-ineffective and complicated instruments. Thus, there is an urgent need to establish an easy and efficient detection method for thiophenols without dependence on the expensive and complex instruments.

Colorimetric detection involves an easy visualization of the color change by naked eye and does not rely heavily on sophisticated instruments. For instance, Yoon et al. constructed a phenolphthalein-based colorimetric probe for thiophenols detection by using 2, 4-dinitrophenyl ether as the recognition unit. However, the probe can distinguish thiophenols from other aliphatic thiols only under alkaline condition [5]. Besides, 2, 4-dinitrobenzenesulfonamide (DNBS) group has been selected as the biologically active moiety for constructing probes of thiophenols, due to its specific reaction site for thiophenols instead of biothiols under normal physiological conditions [6,7]. On the other side, carbon-based nanomaterials exhibit excellent physical and chemical properties [8,9], and carbon dots (CDs) have been successfully used to construct nanoprobes for detection of small molecules.

In the present work, a colorimetric turn-on nanoprobe was constructed for detecting thiophenols via surface functionalization of CDs with a highly specific thiophenol (PhSH) biorecognition element. Notably, the yellow-emissive CDs (Y-CDs) synthesized from o-phenylenediamine (o-PD) and urea were used as the colorimetric reporter, while DNBS group was selected as the recognition unit to afford the 2, 4-dinitrobenzene-functionalized CDs (CD-DNBS) as a novel nanoprobe. CD-DNBS was nearly colorless. Upon addition of PhSH solution, the DNBS moiety of CD-DNBS could be readily cleaved via nucleophilic substitution reactions, leading to an obvious color change to yellow (Fig. 1 ). The proposed nanoprobe displays outstanding specificity towards PhSH over biothiols.

o-PD, urea, 2, 4-dinitrobenzenesulfonyl chloride, metal ions and biothiols were supplied by Aladdin Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All reagents were of analytical grade and used without further purification.

The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was carried out using an ESCALAB 250Xi XPS spectrometer equipped with multi-technique capability. Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was conducted using a Tensor 27 FTIR spectrometer. The absorbance spectra were measured with a Shimadzu UV-2550 spectrophotometer.

Many methods have been developed to synthesize functional nanoparticles [10,11], and the Y-CDs were prepared through a solvothermal method according to reference [12,13]. A mixture of Y-CDs (20 mg) and 2, 4-dinitrobenzenesulfonyl chloride (200 mg) was stirred at room temperature for 2 h in anhydrous CH2Cl2 (6 mL). Then, the solution was washed with water, dried over anhydrous MgSO4, filtered, and evaporated under reduced pressure. The crude residue was further purified by silica gel flash column chromatography using CH2Cl2/CH3OH (10: 1, v/v) as eluents to afford CD-DNBS.

To prepare the stock solution, 10 mg of CD-DNBS were dissolved in 10 mL acetone. Different concentrations of PhSH were mixed with CD-DNBS (1 mg/mL, 20 μL) and phosphate buffer (40 mM, 0.5 mL, pH 7.4). The reaction mixture was diluted to 1.0 mL, followed by a proper mixing. Finally, the absorbance spectra were measured.

Compared with Y-CDs [12], CD-DNBS was determined to have a new element of S 2p at 164 eV (Fig. 2 A). Moreover, the high-resolution XPS spectral analysis of N 1 s revealed a significant increase in the content of Nitro N (Fig. 2B), while S 2p band exhibited a characteristic peak, representing S = O (Fig. 2C) [14,15]. CD-DNBS was further evaluated by FTIR analysis, and the absorption bands of − NO2 (1541 and 1353 cm−1) and − SO2− (1378 and 1167 cm−1) were detected (Fig. 2D). These findings strongly indicated that DNBS was successfully coated on the surfaces of Y-CDs.

The kinetic behaviors of CD-DNBS toward PhSH were evaluated (Fig. 3 A). The unbounded probe displayed no absorption peak at 400 nm. Upon the incubation of CD-DNBS with PhSH, the absorption band at 400 nm was increased with time and achieved a plateau at 10 min. Then, the quantitative capability of CD-DNBS for PhSH detection was evaluated. Upon progressive exposure to PhSH (0–70 μM), a distinct color change was visualized (Fig. 4 B). Moreover, after exposure to 70 μM PhSH, the absorbance of CD-DNBS solution at 400 nm elevated gradually until reaching a plateau (Fig. 3B). In addition, the absorption bands of CD-DNBS at 400 nm were linear against PhSH concentrations within the range of 0–40 μM (Fig. 3C), and the detection limit was calculated to be 0.15 μM (3σ/k). Altogether, these findings that CD-DNBS can be used as a novel type of nanoprobe for PhSH determination.

To explore the mechanism for the response to PhSH, the absorption spectra of the final product and Y-CDs were investigated (Fig. 3D). The absorption maximum of final product and Y-CDs were respectively located at 400 and 415 nm, and the fluorescence intensity was not recovered upon the incubation of CD-DNBS with PhSH in aqueous solution (inset of Fig. 3D). As the surface modification changing the surface state of Y-CDs contributes to the fluorescence quenching, the above phenomenon may be due to the fact that the surface-coated DNBS moiety was not totally cleaved by PhSH via nucleophilic substitution reactions and the Y-CDs were not afforded as the final product (Fig. 1) [15].

The selectivity of CD-DNBS towards PhSH was assessed by comparing with other biologically related species. Various analytes such as metal ions, amino acids, reducing agents and biothiols were not obviously detected by CD-DNBS under identical conditions (Fig. 4A). These results indicate that the nanoprobe exhibits the highest selectivity towards PhSH over other biological species. Such high selectivity of CD-DNBS towards PhSH suggest that the sulfonamide connection is primarily exists on the surfaces of CD-DNBS.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of PhSH via the test strip
ce:section.ce:para: To make the detection experiments easy to perform and more practical, CD-DNBS was applied for detecting PhSH via the test strip. First, filter papers were soaked in an acetone solution containing 1 mM CD-DNBS, and the test strips were formed. Then, the test strips were placed into different concentrations of PhSH solutions. Before visualization, the test strips were dried in the air. With the increase of PhSH concentrations, the color of the strips changed from white to yellow (Fig. 4B). These findings reveal that CD-DNBS can be used for monitoring thiophenols in real environmental samples.

A comparison of methods for detection of PhSH has been performed (Table S1). Previous probes exhibit excellent merits in PhSH sensing, such as large Stokes shift, high sensitivity and selectivity [5,16–18]. However, there were several disadvantages that could not be ignored, such as relatively complex synthesis procedures, poor photostability, and rely heavily on sophisticated instruments. In contrast, our nanosensor has been successfully applied to the precise measurement of PhSH by naked eyes, which were superior to the above probes.

In conclusion, a novel colorimetric nanoprobe for PhSH was developed by using Y-CDs as the colorimetric reporter. The recognition unit DNBS group was covalently conjugated on the surfaces of Y-CDs to afford CD-DNBS as a highly selective and sensitive nanoprobe for detecting PhSH. The established probe displays a high selectivity towards PhSH over other biologically related species and is applicable to the precise measurement of PhSH by the test strip. It is speculated that this novel CD-based nanoprobe can provides a basic framework for the detection of other biological analytes by incorporating various biorecognition elements.
Shengrui Zhang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision, Writing - review & editing. Qin Wang: Investigation, Data curation, Writing - original draft. Zhifeng Liu: . Jin Liu: . Jiufu Lu: .

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the financial support by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21807068), the Natural Science Foundation of Shaanxi Province (No. 2019JQ060, 2020KJXX-030), the Educational Scientific Research Project of Shaanxi Province of China (20JK0575, 18JS023), the Startup Project of Shaanxi University of Technology (SLGRC12, SLGRCQD2007).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matlet.2020.128390.
